title: Decoder q8/bf16 normalization distribution robustness
hypothesis: The measured bf16 reciprocal-normalization datapath is the current fastest row-8 Nangate45 point, but q8 reciprocal normalization may still be attractive if its quality is more stable across distribution shifts or if area/power balance dominates timing. The next decision should test the same q8 exact, q8 reciprocal, and bf16 reciprocal normalization grid on broader prompt/logit distributions before treating the prompt-stress frontier as architecture guidance.

direct_comparison.primary_question: Across the distribution dataset, do bf16 reciprocal normalization and q8 reciprocal q10/q12/q14/q16 preserve exact next-token and top-k behavior relative to the reference and q8 exact normalization baseline?
include: distribution dataset reference and candidate regeneration
measured q8 reciprocal, q8 exact, and bf16 reciprocal Nangate45 datapath PPA artifacts
exclude: new RTL generation or OpenROAD measurements
changing mapper or NPU architecture points
follow_on_broad_sweep: add weight/model-family diversity if the distribution dataset preserves multiple exact-safe normalization candidates
narrow the PPA/quality tradeoff only after distribution-sensitive failures are visible

expected_benefit: separates prompt-stress quality from broader distribution robustness
keeps q8-versus-bf16 normalization ranking grounded in measured PPA instead of heuristic costs
identifies whether q8 reciprocal precision is sensitive to input distribution before deeper datapath tuning
risks: the broader dataset still uses the same tiny decoder model
software-emulated quantization may miss some hardware rounding behavior
the measured PPA points are row-8 Nangate45 datapath evidence, not a full decoder macro implementation
